I've written a new book. 
It's about the journey into manhood.
A man is a choice, not a certainty.
A man cannot just be someone who reaches a certain age, and that alone.
That much is too easy.
Manhood is not granted automatically by time. 
To be a man must be earned.
In these pages, I explore what it is that makes a man—the responsibility he carries to himself and others.
This is for the teenage boy trying to understand himself, for the parents raising boys into men, and for those hoping to find the love of a man and wondering what to expect. 
This is for the boys growing up without fathers.
And, selfishly, it is also for me.
You can get your copy now from Amazon 
Building the Man: The Anvil of Becoming

caption: Are Women Struggling With P*rn?
firstComment:
I was at a major university during a peer review study, and I helped out with some of the data. Over 475,000 people participated. About 68 percent were women, the rest men.

What surprised me was how many women, especially between 18 and 35, reported watching porn regularly. A large number said they watch it daily, and a lot of them use it as a way to relax or unwind before going to sleep.

I didn’t expect the numbers to be that high.

caption:
Post-Content Clarity.

Taken from my 3 Minute Monday newsletter, sign up at chriswillx.com/books
firstComment: Social media can become a self development tool when you manage the profiles you follow and the content you consume. You are what you eat 😉🧠

latestComments.text: Social media can become a self development tool when you manage the profiles you follow and the content you consume. You are what you eat 😉🧠
On X. I just follow the ones I want and never go to "For you". It's like having those people as neighbours.

I’ve been thinking along similar lines just lately. Any one of the multiple posts I see in a day from the good creators I follow would give me the inspirational message or advice or just plain old kick up thr arse to go and change my day, my thinking maybe even my whole life if I followed it’s message exclusively. But no there is that compulsion either to push on  and see if there’s another golden nugget in the next post and the next to match the one that hooked you. Within the minute you’ve forgotten that post. Repeat.
10/10 recommend heavy consumption of cute dogs + lifting content for this very reason

Post content clarity! Brilliant. I know exactly how I feel after listening to some podcasts. Some make me feel like crap 50% of the time, while others make me feel great 99% of the time… never thought about it like that. So true. This podcast, 
@timferriss , @hubermanlab , @danharris , @wemightbedrunk , @simonsinek @solvedpodcast @thediaryofaceopodcast are so solid. 99% of the time makes me so happy

alt: Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, poster, card and text that says 'Post-Content Clarity. This is the best way to work out whether the content you consume is making your life better or worse. You see, there is a problem. While you watch anything, you are distracted by the content itself. Which means you can't judge how the content impacts you.'.

childPosts.alt: Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine, poster, card and text that says 'Post-Content Clarity. This is the best way to work out whether the content you consume is making your life better or worse. You see, there is a problem. While you watch anything, you are distracted by the content itself. Which means you can't judge how the content impacts you.'.

Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be a black-and-white image of text that says 'The creator has designed the content to be compelling and to keep you hooked, because if they didn't, they'd be beaten by another creator who did that better. But just because something is compelling, doesn't mean it's good for you.'.

Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be an image of poster, magazine and text that says 'But once you finish consuming you forget that you consumed it, move on with your life and don't assess whether it was actually good for you. In this This way, you are like shop owner in a shop with no walls you allow your most valuable resource (your attention) to be stolen by whichever individuals are most bold and aggressive then tomorrow you forget that they didn't y you and allow them to do it all over again.'.

Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be an image of poster and text that says 'You hate-watch adversarial, argumentative videos, not because there is a fascinating question being answered, but because you want to see your team make the other team look silly. You pity-follow accounts to check in on the slow motion car crash of whatever catastrophe is happening to that person's existence. You descend into scroll-holes scroll- -holes and browse Twitter arguments as your heart rate gets jacked up through the roof in silent apoplectic indignation.'.
Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be a graphic of poster, magazine and text that says 'The solution is to ask yourself this: How does watching different creators make you feel?'.

Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be a black-and-white image of poster and text that says 'Some YouTube channels are compelling and limbically hijacking and keep me watching, but feel uptight and tense and negative or cynical and zero- sum after watching them. don't want to message my friends and tell theml miss them or pay people compliments or go outside and see nature. I feel like the world is against me. That's not the sort of content want to consume any of, no matter how much it makes my dopamine fire.'.

Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'On the other hand, what is the content that makes you feel most connected to the world? What makes you feel hopeful, open, prepared, informed, light and aligned? If your body is made up of things you put into your mouth, your mind is made of things you put into your eyes and ears.'.

Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be an image of poster, magazine and text that says 'To achieve freedom you must be able to think for yourself. If you don't step in and live intentionally, the best you can hope for is to become a rich, successful or famous slave. A slave to your base instincts, the worst norms of the society around you and the confused chemical signals of your body.'.

Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be an image of crossword puzzle and text that says 'You will spend your life focused on unhealthy aims defined for you by others and the worst parts of yourself. You will pass these bad assumptions about life onto your children and loved ones. Worst of all, you will reinforce these boring, desperate defaults in everyone you encounter.'.

Photo by Chris Williamson on April 02, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'If Ifyou yu never peer into your programming then you may end up being the cleverest rat in the room, but that's hardly worth celebrating.'.

caption:
#repost @circle.top

• • •

Alex Hormozi is a first-generation Iranian-American entrepreneur, investor, and philanthropist. In 2013, he started his first brick & mortar business. Within three years, he successfully scaled his business to six locations. He then sold his locations to transition to the turnaround business. From there he spent two years turning 32+ brick & mortar businesses around using the same model that made his privately owned locations successful.

After that experience, he packaged his process into a licensing model which scaled rapidly in 4 years. Over that same four-year period, he founded and scaled three other companies to $120M+ in cumulative sales across four different industries (software, service, e-commerce, and brick & mortar) without taking on outside capital. He has scaled and exited 7 companies. His most notable exit was his majority sale of his licensing company for $46.2M in 2021.

He transitioned from CEO to the owner/shareholder position in these companies in 2020, and founded Acquisition.com as a way to invest his own wealth (both monetary and intellectual capital) into other businesses. The portfolio is based on his investment thesis focusing on asset-light, high cash flow, sales-focused service and digital products businesses.

He is known for his expertise in customer acquisition and monetization.

He donates much of his free time to advancing equal access to education and encouraging entrepreneurship in underprivileged communities.

Alex graduated from Vanderbilt University Magna Cum Laude in three years with a BS in Human & Organizational Development with a focus on Corporate Strategy. He worked as a management consultant for a boutique strategy firm for two years after graduating before starting his first business.

#entrepreneur #dontwastetime #Motivation #success

It's about the journey into manhood.
A man is a choice, not a certainty.
A man cannot just be someone who reaches a certain age, and that alone.
That much is too easy.
Manhood is not granted automatically by time. 
To be a man must be earned.
In these pages, I explore what it is that makes a man—the responsibility he carries to himself and others.
This is for the teenage boy trying to understand himself, for the parents raising boys into men, and for those hoping to find the love of a man and wondering what to expect. 
This is for the boys growing up without fathers.
And, selfishly, it is also for me.
You can get your copy now from Amazon 
Building the Man: The Anvil of Becoming
https://amzn.eu/d/0ioTK1Ts

latestComments.text: The most Jewish thing I’ve ever seen 😂
you articulate the way my brainwaves work so beautifully

Maybe I'm mean, but when I say "how are you" I don't mean it literally. It's a polite formality. The only reason people use it literally is because they feel mean otherwise, but that's what it's for. If you need to talk, say "I need to talk," and we'll go out for coffee. If I ask in passing "How are ya" it means I wish youwell but don't need to hear all about it.

alt: Photo by Chris Williamson in Byron Bay, New South Wales with @chrishemsworth. May be an image of one or more people and text.

caption:
#Repost @7daygenius

• • •

Most people are out here trying to “reduce stress” and “find balance” like it’s some achievement.

Meanwhile, they’re so bored with their own lives they need to scroll for 3 hours just to feel something.

That pressure you’re running from? That’s the only thing keeping you sharp. That stress you’re medicating away with Netflix and takeout? That’s your body telling you you’re actually ALIVE.

You think successful people got there by “managing their stress levels”? No. They got addicted to the feeling of having too much to do and crushing it anyway.

Your comfort zone isn’t keeping you safe. It’s suffocating you.

latestComments.text: How do you distinguish stress that builds you up from stress that tears you down?
Exactly! Your goals give you life!
Couldn’t be more right and it’s something I’m trying to live by after running away from it for so many years.
